UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents a user's profile information, separate from authentication details, linked to an external authentication system. This entity stores core user data needed by the application.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity.

email.description: The user's email address, primarily used for identification and communication, linked to an external authentication system. This field has an 'email' format.

displayName.description: A user-friendly name displayed within the application, such as in personalized greetings.

UserAccount.title: UserAccount
UserAccount.description: Stores a user's financial balances, including the main balance ('Saldo') and bonus balance ('Bônus'), which are critical for the application's functionality.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserAccount entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile associated with this financial account. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N UserAccount)

balance.description: The current main monetary balance available to the user ('Saldo'). This value is persistently stored and updated in real-time.

bonusBalance.description: The current bonus monetary balance available to the user ('Bônus').

terminalId.description: Reference to the Terminal where the account is currently or last accessed. This represents the 'Terminal 1234' concept in the UI. (Relationship: Terminal 1:N UserAccount)

lastUpdated.description: Timestamp indicating when the account balances were last updated. This field has a 'date-time' format.

Terminal.title: Terminal
Terminal.description: Represents a physical or logical terminal through which users interact with the system, such as 'Terminal 1234'.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Terminal entity.

name.description: A human-readable name for the terminal (e.g., 'Terminal 1234'), displayed in the user interface.

location.description: Optional description of the terminal's physical or logical location.

definition.description: Stores individual user profile details. The `userId` parameter matches `request.auth.uid`, ensuring private ownership and simplifying security rules.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user, matching Firebase Authentication's UID.

definition.description: Stores financial balances (main and bonus) for a specific user. The `userId` in the path ensures direct authorization for the owning user. The document also includes the `userId` field from the schema, acting as denormalized authorization context for redundancy and integrity.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who owns this account, matching Firebase Authentication's UID.

params.name: userAccountId
params.description: The unique identifier for the user's specific account document.

definition.description: Stores details about physical or logical terminals. These are likely globally readable for all authenticated users, with write access restricted to administrators.

params.name: terminalId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific terminal.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore data structure for LotoHub prioritizes security, scalability, and debuggability by strictly adhering to the core design principles. The system is designed around user-centric data, ensuring that personal information and financial accounts are securely isolated while shared resources like terminals are globally accessible under controlled permissions. Authorization is primarily driven by path-based ownership and denormalization, eliminating the need for complex and brittle `get()` calls in security rules.

**Authorization Independence (CRITICAL)**: This structure achieves authorization independence by placing user-specific data directly under the authenticated user's ID. For instance, `UserProfile` is stored at `/users/{userId}`, and `UserAccount` data at `/users/{userId}/userAccounts/{userAccountId}`. In both cases, the `{userId}` in the path directly corresponds to `request.auth.uid`. This allows security rules to simply check `request.auth.uid == userId` without needing to fetch any parent documents or even the document itself to determine ownership. For `UserAccount` documents, the `userId` field is also included in the document, effectively serving as a denormalized attribute that reinforces ownership and provides a consistent authorization context within the document itself, even though the path already guarantees it. This ensures atomic operations (transactions/batches) are robust as authorization doesn't depend on potentially outdated parent data.

**Clarity of Intent (Debuggability)**: The path structure clearly delineates data ownership. `/users/{userId}` explicitly states that all data within this subtree belongs to or is managed by the user with that `userId`. This makes security rules simple and highly readable, such as `allow read, write: if request.auth.uid == userId;`.

**DBAC (No Custom Claims)**: All authorization roles or permissions (though not explicitly required for this initial design beyond simple ownership) would be stored as data in Firestore, such as a `/roles_admin/{uid}` collection, rather than relying on Firebase Auth custom claims.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters)**: The design inherently supports secure `list` operations. For example, a user can securely query their own accounts by listing documents in `/users/{request.auth.uid}/userAccounts`. The rules prevent them from listing accounts belonging to other users. For `Terminal` data, which is likely globally readable, a `list` operation on `/terminals` would return all terminals, which is the intended behavior. There is no mixing of public and private data in a single collection that would necessitate filtering rules on read.

**Structural Segregation**: Each collection or subcollection (`/users`, `/users/{userId}/userAccounts`, `/terminals`) holds documents with a homogeneous security posture. User profiles and accounts are strictly user-owned, while terminals are a shared, likely read-only resource for authenticated users. This simplifies rules significantly.

**Access Modeling**: Path-based ownership (`/users/{userId}`) is used for `UserProfile` and `UserAccount`. `Terminal` is a top-level collection (`/terminals/{terminalId}`) as it's a shared resource that is referenced by `UserAccount` but not owned by a specific user.

Overall, this structure provides a robust foundation for LotoHub, ensuring that user data is secure and accessible only by its rightful owner, while common data is managed appropriately, all contributing to a maintainable and debuggable security ruleset.
